verses.text: David tells the assembly the work is great and the king young, then gives lavishly from his own treasure 'because I have set my affection to the house of my God,' and asks, 'who then is willing to consecrate his service?' He leads by costly example. True devotion gives before it asks.

David tells the assembly the work is great and the king young, then gives lavishly from his own treasure 'because I have set my affection to the house of my God,' and asks, 'who then is willing to consecrate his service?' He leads by costly example. True devotion gives before it asks.

David tells the assembly the work is great and the king young, then gives lavishly from his own treasure 'because I have set my affection to the house of my God,' and asks, 'who then is willing to consecrate his service?' He leads by costly example. True devotion gives before it asks.

David tells the assembly the work is great and the king young, then gives lavishly from his own treasure 'because I have set my affection to the house of my God,' and asks, 'who then is willing to consecrate his service?' He leads by costly example. True devotion gives before it asks.

The leaders offer willingly, gold and silver in abundance, and 'the people rejoiced, for that they offered willingly... with perfect heart.' Glad, whole-hearted giving fills them with joy. God loves a cheerful giver, and generous devotion is its own gladness.

David blesses the LORD: 'Thine, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory... thine is the kingdom.' He ascribes everything to God. The fitting response to His goodness is to crown Him with praise as supreme over all.

David blesses the LORD: 'Thine, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory... thine is the kingdom.' He ascribes everything to God. The fitting response to His goodness is to crown Him with praise as supreme over all.

David blesses the LORD: 'Thine, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory... thine is the kingdom.' He ascribes everything to God. The fitting response to His goodness is to crown Him with praise as supreme over all.

'But who am I, and what is my people... for all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee,' confessing they are 'strangers' whose days are 'as a shadow.' We can give God only what He first gave us. Humility, not pride, marks the truest giving.

'But who am I, and what is my people... for all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee,' confessing they are 'strangers' whose days are 'as a shadow.' We can give God only what He first gave us. Humility, not pride, marks the truest giving.

David prays God would keep this willing heart in the people forever and give Solomon 'a perfect heart' to keep His commandments. He intercedes for the next generation's devotion. The best legacy we can seek for our children is a heart wholly set on God.

David prays God would keep this willing heart in the people forever and give Solomon 'a perfect heart' to keep His commandments. He intercedes for the next generation's devotion. The best legacy we can seek for our children is a heart wholly set on God.

All the assembly blesses the LORD and worships, offering a thousand bullocks, rams, and lambs, and eating before Him with great gladness. Worship overflows into joyful feasting. The presence of God among a united people is cause for deep rejoicing.

Solomon sits 'on the throne of the LORD' as king, and all Israel obeys him, the LORD magnifying him exceedingly. The throne is God's, the king His vice-regent. Earthly authority is held under God, and He exalts whom He wills for His purposes.

Solomon sits 'on the throne of the LORD' as king, and all Israel obeys him, the LORD magnifying him exceedingly. The throne is God's, the king His vice-regent. Earthly authority is held under God, and He exalts whom He wills for His purposes.

Solomon sits 'on the throne of the LORD' as king, and all Israel obeys him, the LORD magnifying him exceedingly. The throne is God's, the king His vice-regent. Earthly authority is held under God, and He exalts whom He wills for His purposes.

David, having reigned forty years, dies 'in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honour,' and Solomon reigns in his place. A life lived, in the main, for God ends in fullness and peace. The man is buried, but God's purpose moves on to the next generation.
